mnemonics.title: OS Services Categories
mnemonics.content: Use mnemonic: 'UP IPF CEAR' - User interface, Program execution, I/O operations, File manipulation, Communication, Error detection, Resource allocation, Protection/Security.

mnemonics.title: System Call Types
mnemonics.content: Use mnemonic: 'PFID PEDI' - Process control, File management, Information maintenance, Device management, Communication, Protection. Or 'The 6 P's: Process, File, Info, Device, Comm, Protection.

acronyms.usage: Higher-level functions that programs use to interact with OS, often wrapping multiple system calls.
Text-based user interface where users type commands (e.g., Unix shell, Windows CMD).
Visual interface with icons, windows, and mouse interaction (e.g., Windows, macOS).
Mechanisms for processes to communicate: pipes, message queues, shared memory, sockets.
Unique number assigned to each process by the OS for tracking and management.
Integer handle used by processes to reference open files/devices.
Flag used in open() system call to open a file for both reading and writing.

visualTips.visualization: Think of a door between user space (house) and kernel space (security office). System call is like requesting a service through an intercom - you press button (invoke), security guard verifies (kernel mode), performs task, and returns result.
Think of file descriptors as numbered locker keys. When you open a file, you get a key (fd). Use that key to read/write (access locker). Close returns the key.
fork() is like photocopying a document - you have one original (parent), and you get an exact copy (child). Both can work independently but started identical.
exec() is like swapping the contents of a bottle - the bottle (process) remains, but what's inside (program) changes completely.

analogies.analogy: OS services are like hotel staff: concierge (I/O), room service (program execution), receptionist (user interface), security (protection), housekeeping (error detection). You (application) request services without worrying about how they're done.

System call is like ordering food in a restaurant. You (user program) tell waiter (system call interface) what you want. Waiter goes to kitchen (kernel), chef prepares food (performs operation), waiter returns with your order (result). You never enter kitchen.
Kernel mode is like CEO's office - only authorized personnel (OS kernel) can access sensitive resources. User programs are employees who must request access through proper channels (system calls).
